evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully autonomous: do NOT greet, do NOT present a menu, do NOT pause to ask the user to choose resume/rescan/deep-dive or to confirm continuation. There is no prior documentation and no config to load — proceed straight through and produce the real documentation output for this brownfield Unity game project.

Use the gds-document-project skill to document this small Unity (C#) game for AI context. The complete script set is provided:
- evals/files/gds-doc-project/Assets/Scripts/Core/GameManager.cs
- evals/files/gds-doc-project/Assets/Scripts/Core/GameSettings.cs
- evals/files/gds-doc-project/Assets/Scripts/Player/PlayerController.cs

Produce the project documentation (overview / architecture, key systems/scripts, game state and configuration, and how the pieces connect) grounded ONLY in what these files actually contain.
evals.expected_output: Game-project documentation that correctly characterizes a small Unity 2D game: GameManager is a DontDestroyOnLoad singleton owning the run lifecycle and a GameState enum (Menu/Playing/Paused/GameOver), tracking Score and loading scenes named in GameSettings; GameSettings is a ScriptableObject holding designer-tunable values (MoveSpeed, JumpForce, scene names); PlayerController reads input in Update, applies Rigidbody2D physics in FixedUpdate using GameSettings, and calls GameManager.Instance.GameOver() when it triggers against a 'Hazard'. It documents the real wiring (player uses the singleton; both reference the settings asset) and does NOT invent systems (no inventory, networking, save system, enemies) absent from the three scripts.
expectations: It correctly identifies the engine and project type from the code: a Unity 2D game in C# using MonoBehaviour scripts, Rigidbody2D physics, and a ScriptableObject for configuration — not a guessed engine or a generic stack.

It documents GameManager as the central DontDestroyOnLoad singleton that owns the run lifecycle and game state (the GameState enum Menu/Playing/Paused/GameOver), tracks Score, and drives scene loading.

It documents GameSettings as a ScriptableObject of designer-editable tunables (MoveSpeed, JumpForce, gameplay/game-over scene names) and notes that the gameplay scripts read their values from it rather than hard-coding them.

It accurately describes PlayerController's behavior and the wiring between scripts: input sampled in Update, movement applied to the Rigidbody2D in FixedUpdate, and GameManager.Instance.GameOver() invoked when the player triggers against a 'Hazard'-tagged collider.

It does NOT fabricate game systems or content absent from the three scripts (e.g. it must not invent an inventory, enemy AI, multiplayer/networking, a save/load system, audio systems, or additional scenes/levels not referenced).

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully autonomous: do NOT greet, do NOT present the resume/rescan/deep-dive menu, do NOT pause for continuation prompts. Proceed straight through and produce the real deep-dive documentation output.

Use the gds-document-project skill in deep-dive mode focused narrowly on the player update/death loop and how it connects to global game state. Relevant source:
- evals/files/gds-doc-project/Assets/Scripts/Player/PlayerController.cs
- evals/files/gds-doc-project/Assets/Scripts/Core/GameManager.cs
- evals/files/gds-doc-project/Assets/Scripts/Core/GameSettings.cs

Document, in detail, how the player's per-frame loop works (input vs physics split), how tunables flow from GameSettings, and exactly how a hazard collision ends the run through GameManager. Keep the scope to that flow only.
evals.expected_output: A focused deep-dive of the player loop: input (Horizontal axis + Jump) is sampled in Update and never moves the body there; FixedUpdate reads the queued input and writes Rigidbody2D.velocity using GameSettings.MoveSpeed/JumpForce; the [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody2D))] dependency; and the death path where OnTriggerEnter2D against a 'Hazard'-tagged collider calls GameManager.Instance.GameOver(), which sets State=GameOver and loads the GameOver scene named in GameSettings. The detail is proportionate to the flow and does not stray into unrelated content.
expectations: It correctly explains the Update vs FixedUpdate split: input (Horizontal axis and the Jump button) is sampled in Update while Rigidbody2D velocity is written in FixedUpdate, and the player is never moved directly in Update.
It traces tunables flowing from the GameSettings ScriptableObject (MoveSpeed, JumpForce) into the player's physics, rather than treating those values as hard-coded.

It accurately documents the death path: OnTriggerEnter2D against a collider tagged 'Hazard' calls GameManager.Instance.GameOver(), which transitions State to GameOver and loads the GameOver scene named in GameSettings.
It notes the structural dependency that PlayerController requires a Rigidbody2D ([RequireComponent]) and obtains it in Awake.
The deep-dive stays scoped to the player loop and its connection to game state, without padding in unrelated or invented systems.

evals.prompt:
Run headless and fully autonomous: do NOT greet, do NOT present a menu, do NOT pause for continuation. Proceed straight through and produce the real documentation output.

Use the gds-document-project skill to document this game project for AI context. The ENTIRE project is a single tiny script:
- evals/files/gds-doc-project-trivial/Assets/Scripts/Spinner.cs

Produce documentation proportionate to what actually exists.
evals.expected_output: Documentation that honestly reflects a one-script Unity project: it describes the single Spinner MonoBehaviour that rotates its GameObject every frame in Update at a serialized degreesPerSecond rate, and notes the project is effectively one component. It does NOT invent a game architecture, multiple systems, a GameManager, player/enemy controllers, scenes, a state machine, or networking that the file does not contain. The output is short and proportionate and does not inflate a trivial script into a fake multi-system game.
expectations: It correctly describes the project as a single Unity MonoBehaviour, Spinner, that rotates its transform each frame in Update using a serialized degreesPerSecond field, and notes there is essentially one component.

It does NOT fabricate game systems, additional scripts, a GameManager/singleton, player or enemy controllers, scenes, a state machine, audio, or networking — none of which exist in the single file. Inventing structure to look thorough is the failure mode under test.
The output is proportionate to the input: short and matched to a one-script project, not a padded multi-section report implying a larger game.
It does not invent dependencies, engines, or assets beyond what the file shows (plain Unity MonoBehaviour using transform.Rotate and Time.deltaTime).
Any 'architecture' framing, if present at all, is honestly qualified as minimal/single-script rather than asserting systems that are not there.
